compliance.title: Compliance Dashboard
compliance.pageDescription: Automated compliance monitoring against SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR frameworks. Checks run in real-time against your current system state — users, devices, posture data, audit logs, and security settings.
compliance.soc2Description: SOC 2 Type II — Service Organization Control. Checks logical access security (CC6.1), encryption in transit (CC6.6), and system monitoring (CC7.2). Required for SaaS companies handling customer data.
compliance.iso27001Description: ISO/IEC 27001 — Information Security Management System. Checks device posture (A.8.1), role-based access control (A.9.4), and cryptographic controls (A.10.1). International standard for information security.
compliance.gdprDescription: General Data Protection Regulation (EU). Checks data encryption (Art. 5(1)(f)), records of processing activities (Art. 30), and access control measures (Art. 32). Applies to organizations processing EU personal data.
compliance.howCheck_soc2_cc6_1_mfa: Queries all active users and calculates the percentage with MFA (TOTP/WebAuthn) enabled. Passes at 100%, warns at 80%+, fails below.
compliance.howCheck_soc2_cc6_6_encryption: All VPN traffic goes through WireGuard tunnels which use Curve25519 + ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption. This check always passes as encryption is enforced at the protocol level.
compliance.howCheck_soc2_cc7_2_audit: Verifies that the audit_log table contains entries, confirming that system events (logins, config changes, access) are being recorded.
compliance.howCheck_iso27001_a8_1_posture: Queries the latest device posture check for each device and calculates how many have a score >= 80. Checks OS updates, disk encryption, firewall, and antivirus status.
compliance.howCheck_iso27001_a9_4_rbac: Counts the number of configured roles in the system. Warns if fewer than 2 roles exist, as this indicates insufficient separation of duties.
compliance.howCheck_iso27001_a10_1_encryption: Queries the latest posture data for each device and checks the disk_encrypted flag. Passes when 100% of devices have full disk encryption.
compliance.howCheck_gdpr_art5_encryption: Verifies that all data in transit is protected by WireGuard's built-in encryption (Curve25519 + ChaCha20-Poly1305). Always passes.
compliance.howCheck_gdpr_art30_audit: Checks that audit logging is active by verifying entries exist in the audit_log table, ensuring records of processing activities are maintained.
compliance.howCheck_gdpr_art32_access: Verifies that role-based access control is in place by counting active users with assigned roles. Always passes when RBAC is configured.
compliance.remediateMfa: Go to Settings → Authentication and enable 'Require MFA'. Then notify users to set up TOTP or register a security key in their profile.
compliance.remediateEncryption: All traffic is encrypted via WireGuard. No action needed.
compliance.remediateAudit: Audit logging should be automatic. If no entries exist, check that the API server is running and database connections are healthy.
compliance.remediatePosture: Devices with low posture scores need attention: ensure OS is updated, firewall enabled, disk encrypted, and antivirus active. Use Devices → Posture to see details.
compliance.remediateRbac: Go to Settings and configure at least admin, user, and viewer roles with appropriate permissions. Assign users to roles via User Management.
compliance.remediateDiskEncryption: Enable FileVault (macOS), BitLocker (Windows), or LUKS (Linux) on all managed devices. Posture checks will detect the change automatically.
compliance.remediateAccess: Ensure all users have assigned roles. Go to User Management and verify role assignments.

ztna.priorityHint: Lower number = higher priority. Policies are evaluated in order.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.heroDescription: Complete guide to deploying, configuring, and managing Outpost VPN — the open-source WireGuard VPN and Zero-Trust Network Access platform.
docs.footer: Outpost VPN Documentation -- Apache 2.0 Open Source

quickStart.title: Quick Start
quickStart.content:
Get Outpost VPN running in under 5 minutes using Docker Compose.

### Prerequisites

- Docker Engine 24+ and Docker Compose v2
- At least 2 GB RAM and 10 GB disk
- A Linux host (or Docker Desktop on macOS/Windows for testing)

### Launch the stack

[code:bash]git clone https://github.com/romashqua/outpost.git
cd outpost
docker compose -f deploy/docker/docker-compose.yml up -d[/code]

This starts **PostgreSQL 17**, **Redis 7**, **outpost-core** (API + UI on port 8080), and **outpost-gateway** (WireGuard on port 51820/udp).

### Verify

Open `http://localhost:8080` in your browser. You should see the Outpost login screen.

architecture.title: Architecture Overview
architecture.content:
Outpost consists of four main components:

- **outpost-core** — Central API server, OIDC provider, admin panel (React SPA), gRPC hub. Serves HTTP on :8080 and gRPC on :50051. All management operations go through core.
- **outpost-gateway** — WireGuard gateway agent. Connects to core via gRPC bidirectional streaming. Manages WireGuard interfaces, applies firewall rules, and handles S2S tunnels. Runs WireGuard on :51820/udp.
- **outpost-proxy** — Public-facing enrollment and auth proxy. DMZ-safe, runs on :8081. Handles device enrollment and auth delegation without exposing core.
- **outpost-client** — VPN client application. Handles authentication (including MFA), WireGuard tunnel management, and device posture reporting.

### Data flow

Clients authenticate via core (or proxy) and receive WireGuard configuration. Gateways maintain a persistent gRPC stream to core for real-time peer updates, health reporting, and configuration changes.

firstLogin.title: First Login
firstLogin.content:
After launching the stack, open `http://localhost:8080` and log in with the default admin credentials:

[code:text]Username: admin
Password: admin[/code]

**Important:** Change the default password immediately after first login via **Users** page or **Settings > Security**.

Once logged in, you will see the **Dashboard** with system overview stats, bandwidth chart, and network topology map.

creatingUsers.title: Creating Users
creatingUsers.content:
Navigate to **Users** in the sidebar and click **Create User**.

Required fields:

- **Username** — unique identifier, alphanumeric + underscores
- **Email** — used for notifications and password resets
- **Password** — minimum 8 characters
- **Role** — admin, user, or viewer

Optional fields: first name, last name, groups.

Users can also be provisioned automatically via LDAP sync or SCIM.

roles.title: Roles (Admin, User, Viewer)
roles.content:
Outpost uses three built-in roles:

- **Admin** — Full access to all settings, users, networks, devices, and gateways. Can manage SSO, compliance, and ZTNA policies.
- **User** — Can manage their own devices, view networks they have access to, and see their own analytics.
- **Viewer** — Read-only access to dashboards and reports. Cannot modify any settings.

Roles are enforced at the API level. Every API endpoint checks the JWT role claim before processing the request.

groupsAcls.title: Groups and Network ACLs
groupsAcls.content:
Groups provide network-level access control:

1. Go to **Groups** in the sidebar
2. Click **Create Group** and give it a name and description
3. Add members (users) to the group
4. Add **Network Access** rules specifying which networks and allowed IPs (CIDR) the group can access

When a device connects, its user's group memberships determine which networks it can reach. ACLs are applied at the gateway level via WireGuard `AllowedIPs`.

ldapSync.title: LDAP/AD Sync
ldapSync.content:
Outpost can synchronize users and groups from LDAP or Active Directory.

Configure in **Settings > Authentication > LDAP / Active Directory**:

- **Server URL** — `ldap://ad.example.com:389` or `ldaps://ad.example.com:636`
- **Bind DN** — e.g. `cn=outpost,ou=service,dc=example,dc=com`
- **Base DN** — e.g. `ou=users,dc=example,dc=com`
- **User Filter** — e.g. `(&(objectClass=user)(memberOf=cn=vpn-users,ou=groups,dc=example,dc=com))`

Sync runs on a configurable schedule (default: every 15 minutes). Users are matched by email or username.

scim.title: SCIM Provisioning
scim.content:
Outpost supports SCIM 2.0 for automated user provisioning from identity providers like Okta, Azure AD, or OneLogin.

### Setup

1. In your IdP, create a SCIM application
2. Set the SCIM base URL to `https://your-outpost-domain/scim/v2`
3. Use a Bearer token generated in **Settings > Integrations**

### Supported operations

- Create, update, and deactivate users
- Group membership sync
- Bulk operations

creatingNetworks.title: Creating Networks
creatingNetworks.content:
Networks define WireGuard subnets that devices connect to.

Go to **Networks** and click **Create Network**:

- **Name** — descriptive name (e.g. "Production", "Office")
- **Address (CIDR)** — network range, e.g. `10.0.0.0/24`. Host bits must not be set.
- **Port** — WireGuard listen port (default: 51820)
- **DNS** — DNS servers pushed to clients (e.g. `1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8`)

Each network can have multiple gateways for redundancy. Devices are assigned IPs from the network's CIDR range automatically.

### Assignment model

Outpost uses a **User → Devices → Network** model:

- One user can have multiple devices
- Each device is assigned to one network (`network_id`)
- The device gets an IP address from its network's CIDR range
- WireGuard config `AllowedIPs` contains the network CIDR (split tunneling)

This allows different devices of the same user to connect to different networks.

gatewaySetup.title: Gateway Setup
gatewaySetup.content:
Gateways are WireGuard endpoints that handle VPN traffic.

### Creating a gateway

1. Go to **Gateways** and click **Create Gateway**
2. Select the target **Network**
3. Enter a **Name** and optional **Endpoint** (public IP/hostname)
4. A **Gateway Token** will be generated — copy it immediately (shown only once)

### Enrolling the gateway

On the gateway host, run outpost-gateway with the token:

[code:bash]outpost-gateway \
  --core-url grpc://core-host:50051 \
  --token "gw_abc123..." \
  --wireguard-interface wg0[/code]

The gateway establishes a gRPC stream to core and begins accepting WireGuard peers.

wireguardSettings.title: WireGuard Settings
wireguardSettings.content:
Global WireGuard settings are in **Settings > WireGuard**:

- **Port** — default listen port for new gateways (51820)
- **MTU** — Maximum Transmission Unit (default: 1420)
- **Keepalive Interval** — PersistentKeepalive in seconds (default: 25)
- **DNS Servers** — default DNS pushed to all clients

These settings apply as defaults. Individual networks and gateways can override them.

addingDevices.title: Adding Devices
addingDevices.content:
Devices can be added in two ways:

### Manual enrollment (admin)

1. Go to **Devices** and click **Add Device**
2. Select the **User** who owns the device
3. Enter a **Device Name**
4. Either paste a WireGuard public key or check **Auto-generate key pair**
5. Click **Create Device**

### Self-enrollment (user)

Users can enroll devices through the outpost-client application or the enrollment proxy. The device appears with status **Pending** until an admin approves it.

approveRevoke.title: Approving / Revoking Devices
approveRevoke.content:
Pending devices must be approved before they can connect:

- **Approve** — Click the approve button on a pending device. The device's WireGuard peer is pushed to all relevant gateways.
- **Revoke** — Revoke a device to immediately remove its peer from all gateways. The WireGuard handshake will fail on next attempt.

Revocation is instant — the peer is removed from the gateway's WireGuard interface in real-time via the gRPC stream.

downloadConfig.title: Downloading WireGuard Config
downloadConfig.content:
After a device is created (with auto-generated keys), you can download the WireGuard configuration:

1. Go to **Devices** and find the device
2. Click the **Download Config** button
3. A `.conf` file is generated with the device's private key, assigned IP, DNS, and gateway endpoint

Import this file into any WireGuard client (official WireGuard app, NetworkManager, etc.).

[code:ini][Interface]
PrivateKey = <device-private-key>
Address = 10.0.0.5/32
DNS = 1.1.1.1

[Peer]
PublicKey = <gateway-public-key>
AllowedIPs = 10.10.0.0/16
Endpoint = gateway.example.com:51820
PersistentKeepalive = 25[/code]

**Important:** `AllowedIPs` contains the CIDR of the network the device belongs to (split tunneling). Only traffic destined for this subnet is routed through the VPN tunnel — all other traffic goes directly.

keyRotation.title: Key Rotation
keyRotation.content:
Outpost's built-in PKI supports automatic WireGuard key rotation with zero downtime.

### How it works

1. Core generates a new key pair for the device
2. The new public key is pushed to gateways via gRPC (added alongside the old key)
3. The client receives the new key and switches over
4. The old key is removed from gateways after a grace period

This ensures continuous connectivity — the device is never disconnected during rotation.

Rotation schedule is configurable in **Settings > Security**. Default: every 30 days.

builtInOidc.title: Built-in OIDC Provider
builtInOidc.content:
Outpost includes a built-in OpenID Connect provider. This allows outpost-client and third-party applications to authenticate using standard OIDC flows.

### Endpoints

- **Discovery**: `/.well-known/openid-configuration`
- **Authorization**: `/oidc/authorize`
- **Token**: `/oidc/token`
- **UserInfo**: `/oidc/userinfo`
- **JWKS**: `/oidc/jwks`

The built-in OIDC provider is used by default for outpost-client authentication.

externalOidc.title: External OIDC (Keycloak, Okta, Auth0)
externalOidc.content:
Connect an external OIDC provider in **Settings > Authentication > OIDC Provider**:

- **Issuer URL** — e.g. `https://keycloak.example.com/realms/outpost`
- **Client ID** — your registered application client ID
- **Client Secret** — the client secret
- **Redirect URI** — `https://your-outpost-domain/auth/oidc/callback`

### Tested providers

- Keycloak 22+
- Okta
- Auth0
- Azure AD / Entra ID
- Google Workspace
- Authentik

saml.title: SAML 2.0 Setup
saml.content:
Configure SAML 2.0 in **Settings > Authentication > SAML 2.0**:

- **Entity ID** — Outpost's SP entity ID (auto-generated)
- **IDP Metadata URL** — URL to your IdP's SAML metadata XML
- **ACS URL** — Assertion Consumer Service URL (auto-generated: `https://your-domain/auth/saml/acs`)

### IdP configuration

In your IdP, create a SAML application with:

- ACS URL from above
- NameID format: `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress`
- Required attributes: `email`, `firstName`, `lastName`

ldapAd.title: LDAP / Active Directory
ldapAd.content:
LDAP authentication allows users to log in with their directory credentials.

Configure in **Settings > Authentication > LDAP / Active Directory**:

- **Server URL** — `ldap://` or `ldaps://` URL
- **Bind DN** — service account for searching users
- **Base DN** — search base for user lookups
- **User Filter** — LDAP filter to match users (e.g. `(sAMAccountName=%s)`)

When a user logs in, Outpost performs an LDAP bind with their credentials. On success, a local user record is created or updated.

mfa.title: MFA (TOTP, WebAuthn, Backup Codes)
mfa.content:
Outpost supports multiple MFA methods:

### TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password)

1. Go to **Settings > Security > TOTP**
2. Click **Enable TOTP**
3. Scan the QR code with an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password, etc.)
4. Enter the verification code to confirm

### WebAuthn / FIDO2

1. Go to **Settings > Security > Security Keys**
2. Click **Register New Key**
3. Follow the browser prompt to register a hardware security key (YubiKey, etc.)

### Backup Codes

Generate one-time backup codes in **Settings > Security > Backup Codes**. Store them securely — each code can only be used once.

### Enforcement

Admins can enforce MFA for all users in **Settings > Authentication > Require MFA**.

wgMfa.title: WireGuard Protocol-Level MFA
wgMfa.content:
This is a key differentiator from other WireGuard VPN solutions.

When WireGuard MFA is enabled, the device's WireGuard peer is **removed from the gateway** when the MFA session expires. This means:

- The WireGuard handshake itself fails — there is no peer to handshake with
- No network packets can flow, even at the protocol level
- Re-authentication (with MFA) is required to re-add the peer

This provides true protocol-level enforcement, not just an overlay. Configure session expiry in **Settings > General > Session Timeout**.

meshVsHub.title: Mesh vs Hub-and-Spoke
meshVsHub.content:
Outpost supports two S2S topologies:

### Full Mesh

Every gateway connects directly to every other gateway. Best for:

- Low-latency requirements between all sites
- Small number of sites (2-10)
- Symmetrical bandwidth needs

### Hub and Spoke

All spoke gateways connect through a central hub gateway. Best for:

- Centralized security inspection
- Many branch offices connecting to HQ
- Asymmetric routing requirements
- Easier firewall management (only hub needs public IP)

creatingTunnels.title: Creating Tunnels
creatingTunnels.content:
### Step-by-step guide

1. Go to **S2S Tunnels** in the sidebar
2. Click **Create Tunnel**
3. Enter a **Name** (e.g. `office-mesh`) and optional **Description**
4. Select **Topology**: Full Mesh or Hub & Spoke
5. For Hub & Spoke, select the **Hub Gateway**
6. Click **Create**

### What happens after creation

The tunnel is created but not yet active — you need to add gateway members (minimum 2). After adding members:

- Core computes WireGuard configurations for each gateway
- Configs are pushed to gateways via gRPC in real-time
- Gateways create S2S WireGuard interfaces and add peers
- Traffic between sites begins routing

### Via API (curl)

[code:bash]curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/s2s-tunnels \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name": "office-mesh", "topology": "mesh", "description": "Office interconnect"}'[/code]

addingMembers.title: Adding Members (Gateways)
addingMembers.content:
After creating a tunnel, add gateway members with their local subnets:

1. Open the tunnel details
2. Click **Add Member**
3. Select a gateway from the dropdown
4. Specify **Local Subnets** (`local_subnets`) — CIDR ranges of the local network behind this gateway (e.g. `10.1.0.0/24`)
5. Click **Add**

Repeat for each gateway. Each gateway must advertise its subnets so other members know which traffic to route through the S2S tunnel.

### Example: three offices

- **gw-moscow**: `local_subnets: ["10.1.0.0/24"]`
- **gw-spb**: `local_subnets: ["10.2.0.0/24"]`
- **gw-nsk**: `local_subnets: ["10.3.0.0/24"]`

After adding all members, core automatically computes WireGuard configurations and pushes them to gateways via gRPC.

For mesh topology, each new member creates N-1 new WireGuard peers. For hub-spoke, each new spoke creates 1 peer to the hub.

routeManagement.title: Route Management
routeManagement.content:
Routes define which subnets are reachable through the S2S tunnel.

1. In tunnel details, go to the **Routes** tab
2. Click **Add Route**
3. Enter the **CIDR** (e.g. `192.168.1.0/24`) and optional description

Routes are exchanged between members using a BGP-lite protocol. When a route is added, all member gateways update their WireGuard `AllowedIPs` and routing tables automatically.

allowedDomains.title: Allowed Domains
allowedDomains.content:
S2S tunnels support domain-based filtering. This lets you restrict which resources on the remote site can be accessed through the S2S tunnel.

### Configuration

1. Open the tunnel details
2. Go to the **Allowed Domains** tab
3. Click **Add Domain**
4. Enter a domain name (e.g. `erp.office-spb.local`)

Domains work in conjunction with DNS resolution on the gateway. If the domain list is empty, all traffic to the member's subnets is allowed.

### Example use case

Restrict access from the Moscow office to St. Petersburg only for:
- `erp.spb.company.local`
- `git.spb.company.local`
- `wiki.spb.company.local`

configGeneration.title: Config Generation
configGeneration.content:
You can generate a ready-made WireGuard config for each gateway member's S2S interface.

### Via API

[code:bash]GET /api/v1/s2s-tunnels/{tunnelId}/config/{gatewayId}[/code]

### Example response

[code:ini][Interface]
PrivateKey = <gateway-private-key>
Address = 10.255.0.1/24
ListenPort = 51821

[Peer]
# gw-spb
PublicKey = <gw-spb-public-key>
Endpoint = spb.vpn.company.com:51821
AllowedIPs = 10.2.0.0/24
PersistentKeepalive = 25[/code]

S2S uses a separate interface (`wg1` / `wg-s2s-*`) and port (51821), isolated from client traffic on `wg0`.

### Automatic delivery

In production, configs are delivered to gateways automatically via gRPC streaming. Manual generation is useful for debugging or for gateways outside the gRPC connection.

s2sClient.title: S2S via outpost-client Gateway Mode
s2sClient.content:
For sites without a dedicated gateway server, outpost-client can run in **gateway mode** to act as an S2S endpoint.

[code:bash]outpost-client gateway \
  --core-url https://core.example.com:8080 \
  --token "gw_abc123..." \
  --advertise-routes 192.168.1.0/24,10.10.0.0/16[/code]

This is useful for small branch offices or home offices that need to advertise local subnets into the VPN mesh.

systemdSetup.title: systemd Unit Setup
systemdSetup.content:
For production S2S deployments, run outpost-gateway as a systemd service:

[code:ini][Unit]
Description=Outpost VPN Gateway
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/outpost-gateway \
  --core-url grpc://core.example.com:50051 \
  --token-file /etc/outpost/gateway-token \
  --wireguard-interface wg0
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
LimitNOFILE=65535

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target[/code]

Enable and start:

[code:bash]sudo systemctl enable --now outpost-gateway[/code]

routeGroups.title: Route Groups
routeGroups.content:
Smart Routes provide selective proxy routing for bypassing network restrictions (similar to dnsmasq + ipset).

### Entry types

- **Domain** — exact domain match (e.g. `example.com`)
- **CIDR** — IP range match (e.g. `203.0.113.0/24`)
- **Domain Suffix** — matches domain and all subdomains (e.g. `.google.com` matches `maps.google.com`, `mail.google.com`, etc.)

Create route groups in **Smart Routes** page. Each group contains multiple entries with the same action.

actions.title: Actions (Proxy, Direct, Block)
actions.content:
Each route entry has an action:

- **Proxy** — route traffic through a configured proxy server. Select which proxy server to use.
- **Direct** — bypass the VPN tunnel and connect directly. Useful for local/trusted services.
- **Block** — drop all traffic to matching destinations. Useful for ad blocking or restricting access.

Routes are evaluated in priority order. The first matching rule wins.

proxySetup.title: Proxy Server Setup
proxySetup.content:
Configure upstream proxy servers in the **Proxy Servers** tab:

### Supported protocols

- **SOCKS5** — standard SOCKS5 proxy
- **HTTP** — HTTP/HTTPS CONNECT proxy
- **Shadowsocks** — encrypted proxy protocol
- **VLESS** — V2Ray VLESS protocol

For each proxy, configure:

- **Name** — descriptive label
- **Type** — protocol type
- **Address** — server hostname or IP
- **Port** — server port
- **Priority** — for load balancing / failover (lower = higher priority)

trustScores.title: Device Trust Scores
trustScores.content:
Outpost computes a trust score for each device based on its security posture. The score (0-100) is calculated from device posture data submitted by outpost-client.

### Trust levels

- **High** (80-100) — device meets all security requirements
- **Medium** (60-79) — minor issues detected
- **Low** (40-59) — significant security concerns
- **Critical** (0-39) — device fails critical security checks

View all trust scores on the **Zero Trust > Trust Scores** tab.

trustWeights.title: Trust Score Weights & Thresholds
trustWeights.content:
Configure how each posture component contributes to the trust score in **Zero Trust > Configure**.

### Default weights (must sum to 100)

- **Disk Encryption** — 20%
- **Screen Lock** — 10%
- **Antivirus** — 20%
- **Firewall** — 15%
- **OS Version** — 15%
- **MFA** — 20%

### Thresholds

Configure the score ranges for each trust level. Enable automatic actions:

- **Auto-restrict** — limit network access for devices below Medium trust
- **Auto-block** — completely deny access for devices below Low trust

conditionalAccess.title: Conditional Access Policies
conditionalAccess.content:
Create fine-grained access policies based on trust scores, user attributes, and network conditions.

Go to **Zero Trust > Access Policies** and click **Create Policy**:

- **Name** — policy identifier
- **Priority** — evaluation order (lower = evaluated first)
- **Action** — Allow, Restrict, or Deny

Policies are evaluated in priority order. The first matching policy determines the access decision. If no policy matches, the default action is Allow.

splitDns.title: Split DNS Rules
splitDns.content:
Split DNS allows routing specific domains to specific DNS servers, useful for resolving internal domains.

Configure in **Zero Trust > Split DNS**:

- **Domain** — the domain to match (e.g. `internal.corp`)
- **DNS Server** — the DNS server to use for this domain (e.g. `10.0.0.1`)
- **Network ID** — which VPN network this rule applies to

When a client queries a matching domain, the query is routed to the specified DNS server instead of the default DNS.

soc2.title: SOC 2 Checks
soc2.content:
Outpost automatically evaluates SOC 2 Type II controls:

- **CC6.1 — MFA** — Checks that all active users have MFA (TOTP or WebAuthn) enabled. Passes at 100%, warns at 80%+.
- **CC6.6 — Encryption in Transit** — Verifies WireGuard encryption (Curve25519 + ChaCha20-Poly1305). Always passes as encryption is protocol-enforced.
- **CC7.2 — Audit Logging** — Confirms that audit log entries are being recorded for system events.

View results in **Compliance > SOC 2** tab.

iso27001.title: ISO 27001 Checks
iso27001.content:
ISO/IEC 27001 information security management checks:

- **A.8.1 — Device Posture** — Checks that devices have posture scores >= 80 (OS updates, disk encryption, firewall, antivirus).
- **A.9.4 — RBAC** — Verifies that multiple roles are configured for proper separation of duties.
- **A.10.1 — Disk Encryption** — Checks that 100% of managed devices have full disk encryption enabled.

View results in **Compliance > ISO 27001** tab.

gdpr.title: GDPR Checks
gdpr.content:
General Data Protection Regulation compliance checks:

- **Art. 5(1)(f) — Encryption** — Verifies data-in-transit protection via WireGuard. Always passes.
- **Art. 30 — Audit Trail** — Confirms records of processing activities via audit log.
- **Art. 32 — Access Control** — Verifies RBAC is in place with proper role assignments.

View results in **Compliance > GDPR** tab.

remediation.title: Remediation Guides
remediation.content:
Each compliance check includes a remediation guide. Click **Fix** on any failed check to see step-by-step instructions.

### Common remediations

- **MFA not enforced** — Go to Settings > Authentication and enable "Require MFA". Notify users to set up TOTP or register a security key.
- **Low device posture** — Ensure devices have: updated OS, enabled firewall, disk encryption (FileVault/BitLocker/LUKS), and active antivirus.
- **Missing audit entries** — Check that the API server is running and database connections are healthy. Audit logging is automatic.
- **Insufficient RBAC** — Configure at least admin, user, and viewer roles. Assign users to appropriate roles.

overview.title: Overview
overview.content:
Outpost supports **multi-tenant architecture** for MSP and enterprise deployments. Each tenant is an isolated organization with its own users, networks, gateways, and device limits.

- **Isolated data** — each tenant's resources are fully separated
- **Plan-based limits** — Free, Pro, Enterprise tiers with configurable user/device/network quotas
- **Subdomain routing** — tenants can be identified by `X-Tenant-ID` header or hostname subdomain
- **Admin management** — global admins manage all tenants from a single dashboard

creation.title: Creating Tenants
creation.content:
Navigate to **Tenants** in the sidebar and click **Create Tenant**.

- **Name** — display name for the organization
- **Slug** — URL-friendly identifier (used for subdomain routing)
- **Plan** — choose Free, Pro, or Enterprise (sets default limits)
- **Limits** — max users, devices, and networks can be customized

[code:bash]curl -X POST /api/v1/tenants \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -d '{"name": "Acme Corp", "slug": "acme", "plan": "pro"}'[/code]

resources.title: Managing Resources
resources.content:
After creating a tenant, assign resources (users, networks, gateways) to it:

- Click on a tenant name to open the detail view
- Use the **Users**, **Networks**, **Gateways** tabs to see assigned resources
- Click **Assign Resource** to add existing resources to the tenant
- Click the unlink icon to remove a resource from the tenant

[code:bash]# Assign a user to a tenant
curl -X POST /api/v1/tenants/{id}/users/{userId} \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'

# Assign a network
curl -X POST /api/v1/tenants/{id}/networks/{networkId} \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'[/code]

plans.title: Plans & Limits
plans.content:
Each plan has default resource limits:

### Free Plan
- 10 users, 20 devices, 2 networks

### Pro Plan
- 100 users, 500 devices, 20 networks

### Enterprise Plan
- 10,000 users, 50,000 devices, 200 networks

Limits can be customized per-tenant via the edit dialog or API:

[code:bash]curl -X PUT /api/v1/tenants/{id} \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -d '{"max_users": 500, "max_devices": 2000}'[/code]

bandwidth.title: Bandwidth Tracking
bandwidth.content:
The **Analytics** page shows real-time and historical bandwidth data.

### How it works

Traffic data is collected automatically:

1. **Gateway** reads WireGuard peer statistics (`rx_bytes`, `tx_bytes`, `last_handshake`) every 30 seconds
2. Stats are sent to **core** via the bidirectional gRPC stream
3. Core records statistics into `peer_stats` and `flow_records` tables
4. The analytics dashboard reads `flow_records` via the REST API

**Important:** Data appears on the chart only after at least one gateway and device are connected. The gateway must be online with an active gRPC connection to core.

### Bandwidth Over Time chart

Displays RX (received) and TX (transmitted) data over selectable time ranges. Data is aggregated per minute, hour, or day depending on the range.

### Metrics

- **Total Traffic** — aggregate bytes transferred
- **Peak Bandwidth** — maximum throughput observed
- **Unique Users** — number of distinct users with active connections
- **Unique Devices** — number of distinct connected devices

topUsers.title: Top Users
topUsers.content:
The **Top Users by Traffic** panel shows which users consume the most bandwidth. Data includes:

- Username
- Total traffic (RX + TX)
- Connection count
- Percentage of total traffic

This is useful for identifying bandwidth-heavy users and capacity planning.

heatmap.title: Connection Heatmap
heatmap.content:
The **Connection Heatmap** visualizes connection patterns over time:

- X-axis: time of day (hours)
- Y-axis: day of week
- Color intensity: number of active connections

This helps identify peak usage times and plan maintenance windows.

prometheus.title: Prometheus Metrics
prometheus.content:
Outpost exposes Prometheus metrics at the `/metrics` endpoint on the core server.

### Available metrics

- `outpost_connected_peers` — current number of connected WireGuard peers
- `outpost_bandwidth_rx_bytes_total` — total received bytes
- `outpost_bandwidth_tx_bytes_total` — total transmitted bytes
- `outpost_gateway_health` — gateway health status (1=healthy, 0=degraded, -1=down)
- `outpost_auth_requests_total` — authentication request counter
- `outpost_s2s_tunnel_status` — S2S tunnel status per tunnel

### Scrape config

[code:yaml]scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'outpost'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['core-host:8080']
    metrics_path: /metrics
    scheme: http[/code]

general.title: General Settings
general.content:
Configure in **Settings > General**:

- **Organization Name** — displayed in the UI header and emails
- **Domain** — primary domain for the Outpost instance (used in OIDC, SAML, email links)
- **Session Timeout** — how long before inactive sessions expire (default: 24 hours). Also controls WireGuard MFA re-authentication interval.

wireguard.title: WireGuard Settings
wireguard.content:
Configure in **Settings > WireGuard**:

- **Port** — default WireGuard listen port (51820)
- **MTU** — packet MTU size (default: 1420, reduce for PPPoE: 1380)
- **Keepalive** — PersistentKeepalive interval in seconds (25 recommended for NAT traversal)
- **DNS** — default DNS servers pushed to clients (comma-separated)

smtp.title: SMTP (Email Notifications)
smtp.content:
Configure email notifications in **Settings > SMTP**:

- **SMTP Host** — mail server hostname (e.g. `smtp.gmail.com`)
- **SMTP Port** — port (587 for STARTTLS, 465 for SSL)
- **From Address** — sender email address

Click **Test SMTP** to send a test email and verify the configuration.

Email notifications are sent for: new device enrollment, MFA setup, password resets, and audit alerts.

webhooks.title: Webhooks & Integrations
webhooks.content:
Configure outbound webhooks in **Settings > Integrations**.

Each webhook receives HMAC-SHA256 signed HTTP POST requests for specified events:

- **URL** — the endpoint to receive events
- **Events** — comma-separated list of event types, or `*` for all events
- **Signing Secret** — auto-generated HMAC key for verifying webhook authenticity

### Event types

`user.created`, `user.deleted`, `device.enrolled`, `device.approved`, `device.revoked`, `gateway.connected`, `gateway.disconnected`, `tunnel.created`, `auth.login`, `auth.mfa_failed`

### Verification

[code:bash]# Verify webhook signature
SIGNATURE=$(echo -n "$BODY" | openssl dgst -sha256 -hmac "$SECRET" | cut -d' ' -f2)
if [ "$SIGNATURE" = "$RECEIVED_SIGNATURE" ]; then
  echo "Valid webhook"
fi[/code]

security.title: Security Settings
security.content:
Per-user security settings in **Settings > Security**:

- **TOTP** — enable/disable Time-based One-Time Passwords
- **Security Keys** — register WebAuthn/FIDO2 hardware keys
- **Backup Codes** — generate one-time recovery codes

Admin-level security policies in **Settings > Authentication**:

- **Require MFA** — enforce MFA for all users (checked by compliance)
- **Session Timeout** — auto-logout duration
- **Password Policy** — minimum length, complexity requirements

dockerCompose.title: Docker Compose
dockerCompose.content:
The recommended deployment method for single-server setups.

[code:bash]# Clone and start
git clone https://github.com/romashqua/outpost.git
cd outpost
docker compose -f deploy/docker/docker-compose.yml up -d[/code]

### Services

- **postgres** — PostgreSQL 17 database (port 5432)
- **redis** — Redis 7 for sessions, pub/sub, rate limiting (port 6379)
- **core** — API server + UI (port 8080 HTTP, port 50051 gRPC)
- **gateway** — WireGuard gateway (port 51820/udp)
- **proxy** — enrollment proxy for DMZ (port 8081)

Migrations run automatically on core startup. The frontend is embedded in the core binary via `go:embed`.

systemdUnits.title: systemd Units
systemdUnits.content:
For bare-metal or VM deployments without Docker:

[code:ini]# /etc/systemd/system/outpost-core.service
[Unit]
Description=Outpost VPN Core
After=postgresql.service redis.service

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/outpost-core
EnvironmentFile=/etc/outpost/core.env
Restart=always
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target[/code]

[code:bash]sudo systemctl enable --now outpost-core
sudo systemctl enable --now outpost-gateway[/code]

helm.title: Helm Chart (Coming Soon)
helm.content:
A Helm chart for Kubernetes deployment is currently in development.

### Planned features

- Horizontal scaling for core (stateless API)
- Gateway DaemonSet for multi-node WireGuard
- PostgreSQL and Redis as sub-charts or external
- Ingress configuration for core and proxy
- PodSecurityPolicy / SecurityContext for WireGuard capabilities

Track progress on GitHub issues.

envVars.title: Environment Variables Reference
envVars.content:
### outpost-core

- `DATABASE_URL` — PostgreSQL connection string (required)
- `REDIS_URL` — Redis connection string (required)
- `HTTP_ADDR` — HTTP listen address (default: `:8080`)
- `GRPC_ADDR` — gRPC listen address (default: `:50051`)
- `JWT_SECRET` — secret for signing JWT tokens (auto-generated if not set)
- `ADMIN_PASSWORD` — initial admin password (default: `admin`)
- `LOG_LEVEL` — logging level: debug, info, warn, error (default: `info`)
- `LOG_FORMAT` — log format: json, text (default: `json`)

### outpost-gateway

- `CORE_URL` — gRPC URL to core (required)
- `GATEWAY_TOKEN` — enrollment token (required, or use `TOKEN_FILE`)
- `TOKEN_FILE` — path to file containing the token
- `WG_INTERFACE` — WireGuard interface name (default: `wg0`)
- `LOG_LEVEL` — logging level (default: `info`)

authJwt.title: Authentication (JWT)
authJwt.content:
All API endpoints (except auth) require a valid JWT token in the Authorization header:

[code:bash]Authorization: Bearer <jwt-token>[/code]

### Obtaining a token

[code:bash]curl -X POST https://outpost.example.com/api/v1/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"username": "admin", "password": "your-password"}'[/code]

Response:

[code:json]{
  "token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIs...",
  "user": {
    "id": "uuid",
    "username": "admin",
    "role": "admin"
  }
}[/code]

If MFA is enabled, the login returns `mfa_required: true` and you must call `/api/v1/auth/mfa/verify` with the TOTP code.

openapi.title: OpenAPI Specification
openapi.content:
Outpost provides a full OpenAPI 3.0 specification at:

`GET /api/docs/openapi.yaml`

You can import this into Swagger UI, Postman, Insomnia, or any OpenAPI-compatible tool for interactive API exploration.

The spec documents all endpoints, request/response schemas, authentication requirements, and error codes.

curlExamples.title: Example curl Commands
curlExamples.content:
### List users

[code:bash]curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  https://outpost.example.com/api/v1/users[/code]

### Create a network

[code:bash]curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name": "production", "address": "10.0.0.0/24", "port": 51820, "dns": "1.1.1.1"}' \
  https://outpost.example.com/api/v1/networks[/code]

### Create a device

[code:bash]curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name": "laptop", "user_id": "uuid", "auto_generate_key": true}' \
  https://outpost.example.com/api/v1/devices[/code]

### Approve a device

[code:bash]curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  https://outpost.example.com/api/v1/devices/{id}/approve[/code]

### Get dashboard stats

[code:bash]curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  https://outpost.example.com/api/v1/dashboard/stats[/code]

vpnClient.title: VPN Client
vpnClient.content: Outpost supports two ways to connect: the native Outpost Client and standard WireGuard clients. The choice depends on your security requirements.

outpostClient.title: Outpost Client (Recommended)
outpostClient.content:
The Outpost Client is our dedicated VPN client with full feature support:

• **2FA/MFA integration** — authenticates directly with the Outpost server, handles TOTP/WebAuthn challenges
• **Automatic key rotation** — keys are rotated transparently without reconnection
• **Device posture reporting** — sends OS version, disk encryption, antivirus status for ZTNA scoring
• **Auto-reconnect** — handles network changes and sleep/wake cycles
• **Multi-network support** — connect to multiple VPN networks simultaneously

Installation:
```bash
# Linux
curl -fsSL https://get.outpost.vpn | sh

# macOS
brew install outpost-vpn/tap/outpost-client

# Windows
winget install OutpostVPN.Client
```

Usage:
```bash
outpost-client login --server https://vpn.company.com
outpost-client connect
outpost-client status
outpost-client disconnect
```

wireguardNative.title: Standard WireGuard Client
wireguardNative.content:
You can use any standard WireGuard client (wg-quick, WireGuard app for iOS/Android/Windows/macOS) with generated .conf files.

**Limitations when using standard WireGuard:**
• No 2FA/MFA support — if MFA is enabled, the standard client **will not work**
• No automatic key rotation
• No device posture reporting (ZTNA features unavailable)
• No auto-reconnect on session expiry
• Works for basic VPN access without MFA

To use:
1. Download the .conf file from the Devices page
2. Import it into your WireGuard client
3. Connect

```bash
# Linux example
sudo wg-quick up ./wg-outpost.conf
```

mfa2fa.title: 2FA/MFA and VPN Clients
mfa2fa.content:
**Important:** When 2FA/MFA is enabled for a user, only the Outpost Client can be used to connect.

How it works:
1. User initiates connection via Outpost Client
2. Client authenticates with username/password
3. Server requests 2FA code (TOTP, WebAuthn, or backup code)
4. Client prompts user for the code and sends it
5. Server issues a time-limited session token
6. WireGuard peer is added to the gateway
7. When the session expires, the peer is **automatically removed** from the gateway — traffic stops instantly

This is the **WireGuard Protocol-Level MFA** feature: the peer is physically removed from the WireGuard interface on session expiry, providing true zero-trust enforcement.

Standard WireGuard clients cannot perform this authentication flow, so they will fail to connect when MFA is required.

**Recommendation:** If you require MFA (recommended for security), deploy the Outpost Client to all users. Use standard WireGuard only for service accounts or environments where MFA is not required.

commonErrors.title: Common Errors
commonErrors.content:
### 401 Unauthorized

Your JWT token has expired or is invalid. Re-authenticate via `/api/v1/auth/login`.

### 403 Forbidden

Your user role does not have permission for this action. Admin role required for most write operations.

### 409 Conflict (duplicate key)

A resource with the same unique field already exists. PostgreSQL constraint violations (error code 23505) are parsed and returned as user-friendly messages.

### 502 Bad Gateway

Core cannot reach the database or Redis. Check `DATABASE_URL` and `REDIS_URL` environment variables and verify the services are running.

wgHandshake.title: WireGuard Handshake Issues
wgHandshake.content:
### No handshake / "Handshake did not complete"

Common causes:

- **Firewall blocking UDP** — Ensure port 51820/udp is open on the gateway host and all intermediate firewalls/NATs.
- **Wrong endpoint** — Verify the gateway's public IP/hostname is reachable from the client.
- **Key mismatch** — The client's peer public key must match the gateway's actual public key. Re-download the config if unsure.
- **Device not approved** — Pending devices have no peer on the gateway. Approve the device first.
- **Session expired (WG MFA)** — If WireGuard MFA is enabled, the peer is removed on session expiry. Re-authenticate.

### Debug

[code:bash]# On the gateway host
sudo wg show wg0

# Check if peer exists
sudo wg show wg0 peers

# Watch for handshakes
watch -n 1 'sudo wg show wg0'[/code]

dbConnection.title: Database Connection
dbConnection.content:
### "connection refused" or "no such host"

Verify the `DATABASE_URL` format:

[code:bash]DATABASE_URL=postgres://outpost:password@localhost:5432/outpost?sslmode=disable[/code]

### "password authentication failed"

Check PostgreSQL user credentials. For Docker Compose, verify `POSTGRES_USER` and `POSTGRES_PASSWORD` in the compose file match `DATABASE_URL`.

### "relation does not exist"

Migrations have not run. Core runs migrations automatically on startup. If running manually:

[code:bash]export DATABASE_URL=postgres://...
make migrate-up[/code]

gatewayConnectivity.title: Gateway Connectivity
gatewayConnectivity.content:
### Gateway shows "offline" in UI

The gateway's gRPC stream to core has disconnected. Check:

- **Core URL** — is the gRPC address (`grpc://core:50051`) reachable from the gateway host?
- **Token** — is the gateway token valid? Tokens are one-time use for enrollment.
- **Firewall** — is port 50051 (or your configured gRPC port) open?
- **TLS** — if using TLS, ensure certificates are valid and trusted.

### Debug

[code:bash]# Check gateway logs
journalctl -u outpost-gateway -f

# Test gRPC connectivity
grpcurl -plaintext core-host:50051 list

# Check gateway process
ps aux | grep outpost-gateway[/code]

tenants.title: Tenant Management
tenants.confirmDeactivate: Are you sure you want to deactivate this tenant? All users will lose access.
tenants.name: Name
tenants.noUsers: No users assigned to this tenant
tenants.noNetworks: No networks assigned to this tenant
tenants.noGateways: No gateways assigned to this tenant
tenants.assignHint_users: Select users to assign to this tenant. Assigned users will be scoped to this organization.
tenants.assignHint_networks: Select networks to assign to this tenant. Each network can belong to one tenant.
tenants.assignHint_gateways: Select gateways to assign to this tenant. Each gateway can belong to one tenant.
